title: Intro Computer Music II
description: Principles of digital (and analog) recording, editing, signal processing (sound manipulation), sound analysis, multi-tracking, mixing, synchronization and mastering; sound synthesis techniques; hardware and software synthesizer and sampler design; fundamentals of MIDI; MIDI and audio sequencing; interactive MIDI performance and compositional techniques; performance interfaces; music notation software; the repertoire of computer and electronic music. Class lecture/demonstrations are supplemented by weekly labs and culminate in student projects.
restrictions: Instructor permission is required for this course. Use the "Request Course Section Prerequisite Override" task found on your academics dashboard under the Planning & Registration section to request this permission.

title: Basics of Orchestration
description: Introduces students to the instruments of the European orchestral tradition and to the basics of orchestrating for the woodwind, brass, and string sections of the modern orchestra. Orchestration techniques will be studied through the examination of scores from the literature as well as through the creative application of writing skills.

title: Advanced Orchestration
description: Gives practice in writing for groups of instruments drawn from the contemporary orchestra, wind ensemble, and other established instrumental collections. The work will culminate with writing for full orchestra. Composition for the concert stage will figure prominently in the projects and assignments.
restrictions: Open to Major only - Music Composition. Completion of CMP 251 required.

title: Composition Symposium
description: Composition Symposium (Undergraduate): Composition Symposium is a forum for presentations by guest composers and other speakers; there are also presentations and discussions by the students enrolled in the class. In preparation for each class meeting, students will be expected to familiarize themselves with the available work of our guest composers, to attend student composition performances that are the basis for Symposium discussions, and to prepare adequately for any special topics discussion that may be part of the schedule.
restrictions: Open to Major only - Music Composition

title: Intro Computer Music II
description: Principles of digital (and analog) recording, editing, signal processing (sound manipulation), sound analysis, multi-tracking, mixing, synchronization and mastering; sound synthesis techniques; hardware and software synthesizer and sampler design; fundamentals of MIDI; MIDI and audio sequencing; interactive MIDI performance and compositional techniques; performance interfaces; music notation software; the repertoire of computer and electronic music. Class lecture/demonstrations are supplemented by weekly labs and culminate in student projects.
restrictions: Instructor permission is required for this course. Use the "Request Course Section Prerequisite Override" task found on your academics dashboard under the Planning & Registration section to request this permission.

title: Advanced Computer Music II
description: An intensive survey of advanced software-based techniques of digital recording, editing, synthesis, analysis and resynthesis, signal processing, mixing, spatial localization, ambience and movement, and current developments in the field. Class lecture/demonstrations are supplemented by weekly labs and culminate in student compositional projects.
restrictions: Instructor permission is required for this course. Use the "Request Course Section Prerequisite Override" task found on your academics dashboard under the Planning & Registration section to request this permission.

description: More advanced techniques, emphasis on compound meters (study of Stravinsky's l'Histoire du Soldat), and accuracy of technique and musicality. This is a one year course & must be taken in sequence: CND 213 Intermediate Conducting I / CND 214 Intermediate Conducting II. Permission of instructor required.

description: A comprehensive survey of choral materials suitable for church, secondary education, and college programs. Offered in fall semesters, alternating years between CND 431 and CND 432.

title: English Academic Purposes II
description: A continuation of EAP 101G, this course concentrates on greater mastery of the skills necessary for academic work at Eastman. Students are expected to read with increasingly critical ability, give individual and group presentations, produce longer written assignments, and develop further language sophistication and accuracy. Coursework will focus on oral and written demonstrations of critical and creative thinking; the use of evidence and appropriate citation of sources; and the organization, style, and development of academic essays.

title: Large Instrumental Ensemble
description: Instrumental ensemble for freshmen and sophomores.

title: Chorale
description: Participants in the Treble Chorus form a vocal ensemble of high-voiced singers from across the university community. They explore history and cultures by studying diverse repertoire, sharing their understandings in concert performances scheduled throughout the semester. They also have the opportunity to cultivate healthy vocal production and musicianship skills.

title: Advanced Instrumental Ensemble
description: Instrumental ensemble for juniors and seniors.

title: Collegium Musicum I: Vocal Ensemble
description: Vocal and instrumental ensemble specializing in the performance of Renaissance and Baroque music on period instruments. May be repeated for credit. By audition. (Prerequisite for keyboard players: KBD 443 Keyboard Continuo Realization or permission of the instructor.)

title: Collegium Musicum I: Baroque Orchestra
description: Vocal and instrumental ensemble specializing in the performance of Renaissance and Baroque music on period instruments. May be repeated for credit. By audition. (Prerequisite for keyboard players: KBD 443 Keyboard Continuo Realization or permission of the instructor.)

title: Gamelan Ensemble
description: The Eastman Gamelan Ensemble performs traditional and modern Balinese music, as well as new compositions in the gamelan gong kebyar style. Working with director I Nyoman Suadin students will be taught through the aural tradition without the aide of notation and experience how this method requires players to work together closely in order to perform as one a unified whole. Weekly rehearsals prepare students for performances throughout the year including the annual World Music Series concert. Open to all students regardless of major. May be repeated for credit.

title: West African Drumming Beg
description: ENS 215 (I, II-1) West African Drumming: Beginners: The Eastman Gamelan Ensemble performs traditional and modern Balinese music, as well as new compositions in the gamelan gong kebyar style. Working with director I Nyoman Suadine students will be taught through the aural tradition without the aide of notation and experience how this method requires players to work together closely in order to perform as one a unified whole. Weekly rehearsals prepare students for performances throughout the year including the annual World Music Series concert. Open to all students regardless of major. May be repeated for credit.

title: Mbira Ensemble
description: The Eastman Mbira Ensemble provides a hands-on introduction to the ancient and sophisticated musical tradition of the Shona mbira of Zimbabwe. Visiting Zimbabwean guest artists will also offer students the opportunity to delve more deeply into traditional musical practices and their cultural and spiritual context. Songs are taught aurally so no musical experience or training is required. May be repeated for credit.

title: Trombone Choir
description: Preparation and performance of trombone ensemble music of various styles. Permission of instructor.

title: Tuba Mirum
description: Preparation and performance of all types of tuba ensemble repertory, especially works for tuba ensemble plus a small number of other instruments.

title: Brass Guild
description: The Brass Guild is composed of juniors, seniors, and graduate students selected by audition. This ensemble provides consistent, weekly rehearsals of brass and orchestral repertoire together with the study of brass orchestral techniques and performances of brass repertoire. Permission of instructor.

title: Horn Choir
description: Preparation and performance of various types and styles of horn ensemble repertory from quartets to octets for 4-24 performers. Prerequisite: concurrent enrollment in HRN 160/460 or permission of instructor.

title: Musica Nova
description: Jazz Ensemble, New Jazz Ensemble, Jazz Lab Band, Saxology. A multifaceted collection of jazz 'big band' experiences that incorporate the study and presentation of jazz from historically significant repertory to new works composed by Eastman student writers. Ensembles accompany renowned jazz soloists, showcase the music of the finest jazz composers and arrangers, and present educational events for audiences across the country. Seating is determined by auditions in the fall. The 70-piece Studio Orchestra (combining Jazz Ensemble and Philharmonia/ESSO for three weeks annually) is periodically organized by assignment; no pre-enrollment is required.

title: Schola Cantorum
description: Jazz Ensemble, New Jazz Ensemble, Jazz Lab Band. A multifaceted collection of jazz 'big band' experiences that incorporate the study and presentation of jazz from historically significant repertory to new works composed by Eastman student writers. Ensembles accompany renowned jazz soloists, showcase the music of the finest jazz composers and arrangers, and present educational events for audiences across the country. Seating is determined by auditions in the fall. The 70-piece Studio Orchestra (combining Jazz Ensemble and Philharmonia/ESSO for three weeks annually) is periodically organized by assignment; no pre-enrollment is required. Same as 6ENS 400J, but for no credit and no charge.

title: Collegium Musicum I: Vocal Ensemble

title: Gamelan Ensemble
description: The Eastman Gamelan Ensemble performs traditional and modern Balinese music, as well as new compositions in the gamelan gong kebyar style. Working with director I Nyoman Suadin students will be taught through the aural tradition without the aide of notation and experience how this method requires players to work together closely in order to perform as one a unified whole. Weekly rehearsals prepare students for performances throughout the year including the annual World Music Series concert. Open to all students regardless of major. May be repeated for credit.

title: Chorale
description: Participants in the Treble Chorus form a vocal ensemble of high-voiced singers from across the university community. They explore history and cultures by studying diverse repertoire, sharing their understandings in concert performances scheduled throughout the semester. They also have the opportunity to cultivate healthy vocal production and musicianship skills.

title: Hollywood Film
description: In this course we will explore “Hollywood” film from diverse perspectives: its history, its style, its business practices, and its power as a force that both mirrors and shapes American society. Students will analyze film sequences and identify how they reflect and revise the “classical” Hollywood style; understand technological, economic and political developments that shape the images on the screen (the coming of sound and color technologies, censorship, the Great Depression, World War II and the Cold War, the representation of racial identities, among others); and examine how film acts as a mediator of social and cultural values.

title: Guitar Pedagogy
description: A course designed to examine the historic and contemporary materials and techniques available for effectively teaching the guitar to students at all levels. The course surveys a wide range of method and studies, and examines the effectiveness of various pedagogical approaches to technique and interpretive analysis. The history of guitar pedagogy is also studied.

Students research the instruments, styles, notation systems, composers, and repertory of the guitar. Professional activities are also addressed. Required of all guitarists in the MM PRL program.

title: Arts Across Rochester
description: How do we study the past? What difference does it make in the present? This course takes a new hands-on approach to the study of modern European and American history and memory by engaging with special arts events across Rochester. Topics will include the First World War, the Great Depression, the Second World War, and the Cold War. Themes will include economic conflict, political polarization, immigration and exile, debates over war and peace, and questions about how to remember the past in the present. Activities will include trips to the Eastman Museum of Photography, the International Theatre Program, the Memorial Art Gallery, and associated virtual exhibits at the Smithsonian Institution, the National Air and Space Museum, and elsewhere.

title: Music, Theater, Cinema and The Nation
description: How did music and theaters contribute to the construction of a nation? In this course we will investigate the pivotal role of Opera (from Bel Canto Opera – Rossini, Bellini, and Donizetti – to Verdiʼs patriotism) in shaping modern Italy and how theaters became a stage to express political dissent. We will examine the process of the unification of the country in cinema (The Leopard and Senso by L. Visconti, Sissi. The Young Empress byE. Marischka, The Great War byM. Monicelli), Verism (Mascagni, Puccini and Verga) and poetry (Ungaretti).We will also learn about national identity, the Mafia and its representation in cinema (Mafioso by A. Lattuada, Johnny Stecchino by R. Benigni). Finally, we will explore national identity and citizenship in contemporary Italy through docu-films (Italy:Love It or Leave It, 18 Ius Solis) and hip-hop culture (from the early hip hop Isola Posse All Stars to contemporary Italian rappers).Therefore, Opera, traditional songs, war experiences, literature, art and films will be analyzed for their role in constructing a nation.

title: Arts Across Rochester
description: How do we study the past? What difference does it make in the present? This course takes a new hands-on approach to the study of modern European and American history and memory by engaging with special arts events across Rochester. Topics will include the First World War, the Great Depression, the Second World War, and the Cold War. Themes will include economic conflict, political polarization, immigration and exile, debates over war and peace, and questions about how to remember the past in the present. Activities will include trips to the Eastman Museum of Photography, the International Theatre Program, the Memorial Art Gallery, and associated virtual exhibits at the Smithsonian Institution, the National Air and Space Museum, and elsewhere.

title: Performance Art
description: Dada, 4ʼ33”, Happenings, “Body Art,” Performance Art: over the last 100 years a mode of performance has emerged that challenges to classical art forms. Provocative, challenging, and sometimes absurd, these performances pressure the boundaries between art and life, representation and reality, audience and stage, sense and non-sense, music and sound, and body and self. In this class we will depart from traditional theater, music, painting, and dance to explore key performance art pieces of the last 100 years. The artists studied include John Cage, Yoko Ono, the Judson Dance Tehater, Chris Burden, Tehching Hsieh, and Marina Abromavic. The course culminates with a survey of contemporary artists active today.

title: The Sensory Life of Infrastructure
description: An abandoned subway? A 200 year-old Erie Canal? Historic bridges? The shuttle system? How do Rochesterʼs infrastructures low-key guide your journey as a music student? The course explores the social, ecological, and aesthetic lives of infrastructure through the lens of critical and creative ethnography. Together we will examine how infrastructures—roads, bridges, water systems, energy grids, subways, and more—shape and are shaped by human and nonhuman life. Through site- specific fieldwork in Rochester, NY, students will trace the life histories of local infrastructures, attending to processes of environmental change, maintenance and repair, experimenting with ethnographic storytelling that blends research and analysis with artistic practice. The course challenges students to see infrastructure not as static background, but as dynamic, contested, and deeply entangled with environmental and social worlds, and to collectively explore these dynamics through the creation and/or performance of critically informed works in various media.

title: Elementary Italian
description: This two-semester sequence is an introduction to Italian language with an emphasis on all four skills: speaking, reading, writing, listening comprehension for students with no previous knowledge of the language. The course will focus on building a basic vocabulary, grammar structures and syntax. Students with prior study of Italian must contact the instructor for a placement test.

title: Elementary Italian
description: Same two-semester sequence as IT 101 and IT 102 with a separate G? designation for graduate students who may take it for one credit. title: Italian Diction
description: This first diction course provides students with theoretical and applied knowledge of the International Phonetic Alphabet, an indispensable tool for their future careers and their future diction courses. Students learn how to apply IPA to the study of Italian Diction through a progressive method based on Italian reading and tongue twisters. They learn how to master proper Italian vowels, consonant clusters, and intonation in prose and poetry through written and oral assignments. This course is not a vocal performing course, but a comprehensive course focused on Italian Diction.

description: This 15-week, 4 unit course provides an immersive study of solo vocal jazz and contemporary music concepts for first-year Jazz Voice JCM students (and/or interested/approved VOVC students), emphasizing performance, performance analysis, band interaction, applied listening, critical discussion, and research. Throughout the semester, students will explore various aspects of jazz vocal performance, including jam session and band-leading techniques, chart writing, swing phrasing, ballad interpretation, bossa nova, improvisation, and more

title: Eastman Jazz Orchestra
description: Jazz Ensemble, New Jazz Ensemble, Jazz Lab Band. A multifaceted collection of jazz 'big band' experiences that incorporate the study and presentation of jazz from historically significant repertory to new works composed by Eastman student writers. Ensembles accompany renowned jazz soloists, showcase the music of the finest jazz composers and arrangers, and present educational events for audiences in Rochester and across the country. Seating is determined by auditions in the fall. The 70-piece Studio Orchestra (combining Jazz Ensemble and Philharmonia/ESSO for three weeks annually) is periodically organized by assignment; no pre-enrollment is required.

title: Eastman Jazz Voice Ensemble
description: Eastman Jazz Voice Ensemble. EJVE is an audition-based, collegiate level CCM/vocal jazz ensemble featuring 12 to 18 vocalists on individual microphones, backed by a student rhythm section. The ensemble experience balances the study and presentation of a wide array of vocal jazz styles, from historically significant repertory to contemporary compositions and even student arrangements. Ensembles accompany renowned jazz soloists, showcase the music of the finest jazz composers and arrangers, and present educational events for audiences across the country. Seating is open to all music and non-music majors, and is determined by auditions held in the spring and fall.

title: Saxology
description: Jazz Ensemble, New Jazz Ensemble, Jazz Lab Band, Saxology. A multifaceted collection of jazz 'big band' experiences that incorporate the study and presentation of jazz from historically significant repertory to new works composed by Eastman student writers. Ensembles accompany renowned jazz soloists, showcase the music of the finest jazz composers and arrangers, and present educational events for audiences in Rochester and across the country. Seating is determined by auditions in the fall. The 70-piece Studio Orchestra (combining Jazz Ensemble and Philharmonia/ESSO for three weeks annually) is periodically organized by assignment; no pre-enrollment is required.

title: Jazz Theory/Improvisation II
description: Exploring both abstract elements and essential functions of improvisation, this course stresses jazz rudiments of chord and scale spellings, chord/scale/mode relationships, jazz nomenclature, basic forms, chord substitution, and chord voicing. Emphasis upon aural training, vocalization, and transcription of recorded jazz solos. Blues structures, modal compositions, and tunes with simple progressions are emphasized in class performance.

title: Functional Jazz Piano
description: A keyboard methods course designed to equip each student with practical basic jazz piano skills sufficient for accompaniment, composing/arranging, and continued study of jazz harmony. Emphasis on basic jazz harmonic formulas, voice leading, scales and modally derived harmonies used in jazz, and two-hand homophonic jazz piano style (right-hand melody, left-hand accompaniment). Lead sheet sight-reading is stressed, in addition to tactile and visual familiarity with the keyboard.
restrictions: Open to Major only - Jazz Studies

title: Functional Jazz Piano

title: Woodwind Doubling: Clarinet
description: Group instruction in basic technique for clarinet. Required for undergraduate JCM saxophone performance majors.

description: Studio Lessons in jazz composition and arranging for undergraduate JCM writing skills majors.

title: Jazz Composition
description: Studio Lessons in jazz composition and arranging for undergraduate JCM writing skills majors.

title: Jazz Pedagogy
description: Basic rehearsal techniques for the development of large and small jazz ensembles. Survey of pedagogical concepts, performance practice, and performance repertory for big bands and small groups. Teaching skills pertaining to improvisation, theory, and aural training from beginning to advanced levels. Hands-on teaching experience and ensemble coaching are essential components in the course. Prerequisite: JCM major or permission of instructor.
restrictions: Open to Major only - Jazz Studies

title: Jazz Arranging I
description: Basic techniques of tune writing are presented and explored in relation to the work of specific jazz composers whose works are rooted in the creative use of tonal harmonic relationships. Students compose original tunes using the musical vocabulary and techniques utilized by specific composers. Prerequisite: for non-JCM majors, JCM 201, 202 or permission of the instructor.
restrictions: Open to Major only - Jazz Studies

title: Jazz Arranging II
description: Contemporary writing approaches for small and large ensembles are presented and explored. The lead sheet works that include modal applications of writers such as Herbie Hancock, Wayne Shorter, Gil Evans, Bob Brookmeyer, Kenny Wheeler etc. are emphasized, along with the analysis and application of modal harmony in jazz. Student will create a portfolio of jazz compositions in both large and small ensemble formats.

title: Early Jazz History
description: Development of compositional and improvisational styles in jazz from 1900 to the present. All periods in the development of jazz are examined. Evolution of specific instrumental styles is also emphasized.
restrictions: Open to Major only - Jazz Studies

title: Jazz Improvisation and Repertoire II
description: Continuation of the content from JCM249. To learn basic jazz improvisation in practice: Improvising over Rhythm Changes, rhythmic syntax, transcribing solos, improvisation over standard tunes: (Speak Low – Weill/Nash, Passport – Parker, Have You Met Miss Jones? – Rodgers/Hart)
restrictions: Open to Major only - Jazz Studies

title: Advanced Jazz Improvisation and Repertoire II
description: To learn advanced jazz improvisation/composition in practice: Transcription, analysis, and performance of the improvisational and compositional languages of modern jazz improvisers from recorded examples: chosen from but not limited to the music of Charlie Parker, Lennie Tristano, John Coltrane, Jerry Bergonzi, David Liebman, Chick Corea, Steve Coleman, Keith Jarrett, Marc Copland, Rich Perry, Ran Blake, McCoy Tyner, Ornette Coleman, Vijay Iyer, etc. Students will perform representative solos and compositions from recordings as well as write and perform their own using the musical principles discovered. Analysis of symmetrical and synthetic scales (hexatonic, octatonic, nonatonic) and their applications to advanced jazz improvisation and composition. Applying constant structures in the creation of complex harmonies for use in jazz improvisation/composition. The study of odd-time signatures and mixed meters in modern jazz improvisation/composition. Polytonality as applied to jazz improvisation.
restrictions: Open to Major only - Jazz Studies

title: Advanced Jazz Improvisation and Repertoire II
description: To learn advanced jazz improvisation/composition in practice: Transcription, analysis, and performance of the improvisational and compositional languages of modern jazz improvisers from recorded examples: chosen from but not limited to the music of Charlie Parker, Lennie Tristano, John Coltrane, Jerry Bergonzi, David Liebman, Chick Corea, Steve Coleman, Keith Jarrett, Marc Copland, Rich Perry, Ran Blake, McCoy Tyner, Ornette Coleman, Vijay Iyer, etc. Students will perform representative solos and compositions from recordings as well as write and perform their own using the musical principles discovered. restrictions: Open to Major only - Jazz Studies

title: Entrepreneurial Thinking
description: An entrepreneurial person is one who transforms an idea into an enterprise that creates value. Musicians have been entrepreneurial thinkers throughout history, and continue to be so. Entrepreneurial Thinking helps students to recognize the entrepreneurial potential they posses, appreciate the role of entrepreneurship within society and in their own professional lives, and understand and implement the processes and skills of entrepreneurship. Students envision, develop, and present a Capstone Project in this course, titled 'The Big Idea'. This is a project, initiative, product, business, or other entrepreneurial idea chosen by the student. Essential concepts covered in this class include idea generation, assessing potential value and feasibility, market analysis, writing for business, developing marketing strategies, budgeting, types of business structures, funding, contracts, legal issues, and best practices for effective presentations. This course may also serve as a resource for students wishing to submit applications to the IML Grant and Mentorship Program, Eastman/ArtistShare Program, and Paul R. Judy Grant Program. Students wishing to take this course for degree elective credit will need to pay for the course and should sign up for JCM 261.

title: Secondary Lute
description: Graduate Applied Music Lessons(one-hour/week): Used to fulfill primary lesson requirement for graduate students. For students who enrolled in their current graduate degree program prior to summer 2009, these lessons carry 5 credits per semester.
restrictions: Open to Primary Instrument - Lute

title: The Idea of Early Music: 800-1750
description: Why perform old music? This course examines this essential question through study of what we now call “early music,” or music in the Western art tradition composed from roughly 800–1750. Unlike the works of Mozart, Beethoven, and other “great” composers of a later era, much “early music” has fallen out of the modern repertory at one point or another, only to be revived again. With every revival, musicians have put old music to new use. This course explores how and why musicians made “early” music across history—at the time it was first composed, and in more recent times. Today, “early music” is alive and well. Students in this course will also deepen their understanding of this thriving aspect of the classical music scene.
restrictions: Open to Juniors and Seniors Only

title: Other People, Other Sounds: 800-1750
description: The people who made and consumed music in what we now call the medieval, Renaissance, and baroque periods of Western music conceived of their worlds very differently than we do today, and indeed very differently from each other. To explore their music we must therefore explore their cultures, philosophies, mental habits. In this course we will consider a series of such intersections of culture and music and ponder how each might inform our understanding of both the technique and meaning of the relevant music. Our recurrent theme will be “Music as Power,” and we will touch on such ideas as the Music of the Spheres, the Birth of Humanism, the Dominion of Rhetoric, the Aesthetics of Wonder, and the Ascendance of Rationalism. Over the course of the semester we will investigate such genres as plainchant, organum, motet, mass, madrigal, opera, sonata, concerto, and more, along with their representative composers and performance practices.
restrictions: Open to Juniors and Seniors Only

title: Music and the Literary Imagination
description: Romantic aesthetics were founded on a paradox: for many listeners, music was ineffable— that is, it prompted experiences so intense as to be beyond description—yet this era also saw an explosion of writing about music, as critics, poets and novelists strove to capture music in words. Composers, too, began to seek inspiration from literary sources in new ways and engage in debates about the proper relationship between the written and the sounded. This course surveys the broad span of (mostly) European music history from 1750 to 1880 through the intersection of words and music. We will focus on musical works that deal with words (song, opera, program music), literary uses of music in novels and poetry, as well as writing about music in the form of reviews and criticism. Students will learn how to understand the major styles and genres of the period in a broader historical and artistic context and will also gain facility in writing about music in shorter forms for today's audiences.
restrictions: Open to Juniors and Seniors Only

title: Musical Culture and Creativity, 1750-1900
description: The “long nineteenth century”, which spanned the late Classical period through Romanticism and early modernism, witnessed a myriad of musical developments. The reigning aesthetic mindset of this era was Romanticism, which valued spontaneity, introspection, and the natural world. How did these ideals shape well-known nineteenth-century genres such as opera, song, choral and chamber music, and symphonies? Moreover, this era featured the rise of the concert hall, virtuoso “superstar” performers, and journalism, as well as the growing involvement and expectations of middle-class audiences. In this course, students will learn about the musical forms, figures, genres, aesthetic trends, and historical events that are central to this era. Students are invited to critically engage with the decision-making of composers, performers, and patrons, seeking to understand how biographical, social, and historical factors ultimately infused the repertoire we perform today. Through vibrant score analysis, group discussions, and creative writing assignments, students will gain a holistic understanding of musical culture in the long nineteenth century, and emerge with an enriched perspective for their continued musical careers.
restrictions: Open to Juniors and Seniors Only

title: Music, Space, and Time since 1900
description: The last 125 years have provoked constant negotiations and re-considerations of musicʼs position in the world. As cultures, technologies, and media mutate and migrate at unprecedented speeds, where and when does music belong? This course explores how composers, performers, audiences, and others have responded to this question from 1900 through today. Through primary sources, secondary scholarship, listening, and writing, we will discuss a wide range of musics and the space-time contexts that enfold them. How might music relate to both the past and the future? How do place and space condition musicʼs production and reception? When, where, and how do imbalances in the cultural, social, and economic powers of music form? We will grapple with these and similar questions to better our understanding of musicʼs role in the 20th and 21st centuries and to practice critical skills that will inform our own complicated musicking.
restrictions: Open to Juniors and Seniors Only

title: Musical Cultures in the 20th Century
description: This class explores currents in global music history from the turn of the 20th century to the present day. In addition to examining biographical details and important works by different composers, this course takes the idea of “culture” as a central point of investigation. Sometimes people distinguish between “capital C” culture (e.g., Beethovenʼs Ninth Symphony) and “little c” culture (e.g., how we relate to our friends and family or how we formulate our dreams and values). For this class, we do not need to think of culture in such binary terms, but instead, we can situate a work like Pucciniʼs Tosca within a range of personal, political, and social forces. Cultures may come into conflict within a nation, a city, or even a classroom. Conversely, cultural values and trends may be shared among individuals around the world who may have never met each other. We will examine musical cultures in all their complexities and contradictions across time and around the world.

Today, “early music” is alive and well. Students in this course will also deepen their understanding of this thriving aspect of the classical music scene.

description: The last 125 years have provoked constant negotiations and re-considerations of musicʼs position in the world. As cultures, technologies, and media mutate and migrate at unprecedented speeds, where and when does music belong? This course explores how composers, performers, audiences, and others have responded to this question from 1900 through today. Through primary sources, secondary scholarship, listening, and writing, we will discuss a wide range of musics and the space-time contexts that enfold them. How might music relate to both the past and the future? How do place and space condition musicʼs production and reception? When, where, and how do imbalances in the cultural, social, and economic powers of music form? We will grapple with these and similar questions to better our understanding of musicʼs role in the 20th and 21st centuries and to practice critical skills that will inform our own complicated musicking.
restrictions: Prerequisite: Successful completion of MHS 123.

title: Musical Cultures in the 20th Century
description: This class explores currents in global music history from the turn of the 20th century to the present day. In addition to examining biographical details and important works by different composers, this course takes the idea of “culture” as a central point of investigation. Sometimes people distinguish between “capital C” culture (e.g., Beethovenʼs Ninth Symphony) and “little c” culture (e.g., how we relate to our friends and family or how we formulate our dreams and values). For this class, we do not need to think of culture in such binary terms, but instead, we can situate a work like Pucciniʼs Tosca within a range of personal, political, and social forces. Cultures may come into conflict within a nation, a city, or even a classroom. Conversely, cultural values and trends may be shared among individuals around the world who may have never met each other. We will examine musical cultures in all their complexities and contradictions across time and around the world.
restrictions: Prerequisite: Successful completion of MHS 123.

title: History of Jazz Styles
description: Investigation of performance and compositional innovations in jazz in the twentieth century. Analysis of scores, transcriptions, and recordings by major jazz stylists.
restrictions: Open to Major only - Jazz Studies

title: Film Music History
description: A study of the key and influential composers of film music from the so-called silent era to the present covering musical trends in all genres.

title: Music in the Renaissance
description: This course surveys western European music from approximately 1430 through the end of the sixteenth century. This period bears witness to shifting social and political changes that impact musical production and reception, including the rise of printing press and of Protestantism. Overviews of the cultural landscape will contextualize the activity of composers and musicians under investigation. Areas of emphasis include the development of vocal genres (motet, Mass, chanson, madrigal). There are also units on race and gender; war and colonialism; and performance practice. The course further covers basic theoretical underpinnings such as mode, hexachord, and notational conventions. Special focus will be given not only to understanding primary musical sources but also to internalizing the art of oral counterpoint, one of the crucial foundations of Renaissance musicianship.

title: Music in the 19th Century
description: This course explores music and musicians in the nineteenth century, with a focus on the Western art music and related traditions in Europe and the United States. In the first half of the course, students learn about key themes in nineteenth-century music and culture (counterculture, desire, community, power, and progress). In the second half, students deepen their understanding of these key themes by exploring more specific case studies. These case studies focus on issues important to nineteenth-century musicians and that remain so today (environment, sovereignty, freedom, equity, belonging). Throughout, we will focus on how musicians used music to express themselves and the values important to them, so that you, as performers, composers, and educators, can decide what you will use this music to say today. The goal of the course is to show you how over a century ago, musicians developed musical styles, institutions, and uses for music that have shaped musical culture ever since and—for better or worse—still shape it now. We study these traditions so that you may transform them.

title: Examining the "Musical Present"
description: This course is designed to provide a solid grasp of twentieth-century European and American art music by offering both broad coverage of significant works and in-depth examination of the era's diverse musical trends, social and political environments, and aesthetic and cultural controversies. Students are expected to contribute to class discussion, engage closely with musical scores, listen attentively to pieces, and write sensitively about compositional details as well as music's multiple roles in contemporary culture. Major graded work includes a midterm, final, and a paper that combines analysis and interpretation.

title: Baroque Perform Practice II
description: Dance music, tempo, ornamentation, improvisation, recitative, Baroque opera practice and a special session on performance practice issues in Bach, based on historical sources.

title: Bibliography
description: A study of sources and reference materials in music.
restrictions: Instructor permission is required for this course. Use the "Request Course Section Prerequisite Override" task found on your academics dashboard under the Planning & Registration section to request this permission.

title: Music in the 19th-Century United States
description: This course examines topics of – and scholarly approaches to - musical culture in the United States during the nineteenth century. Beginning with early American secular and sacred repertories, we will survey consequential figures and genres in search of a realistic picture of musical life in the United States. Areas of focus include musical reform and literacy, performance culture, indigenous musics, theatre and minstrelsy, parlor music, works connected to slavery and the Civil War, and the search for a national music. Running as a thread through the semester will be the dichotomy between individual/elite musics and popular/accessible musics -- and an elusive musical canon. Weekly readings and tasks form a baseline of learning for the course; two presentations and a research project will further be required.
restrictions: Instructor permission is required for this course. Use the "Request Course Section Prerequisite Override" task found on your academics dashboard under the Planning & Registration section to request this permission.

title: German Romanticism, Then and Now
description: The question “What is Romanticism?” is notoriously difficult to answer. Although musicians commonly associate Romanticism with the music of the entire nineteenth century (especially that of the German-speaking lands), the term also refers to literary and philosophical trends that were essentially defunct by 1848, when revolutionary fervor and new perspectives swept across Europe. In keeping with this more restricted timescale, we will seek to gain a better understanding of German Romanticism as an early nineteenth-century phenomenon grounded in ideas that took shape as much around music (or with music as their object) as within it. We will closely study works of music by composers such as Beethoven, Schubert, and the Schumanns that continue to be heard as illustrative of Romantic aesthetics or that invite Romantic listening attitudes, asking what technical features of the music justify these convictions. Finally, through provocative pairings of historical and contemporary readings by authors such as E.T.A. Hoffmann, Andrea Wulf, Brian Massumi, Dalia Nassar, G.W.F. Hegel, Arthur Schopenhauer, Isaiah Berlin, and Hartmut Rosa, we will consider how Romantic notions of nature, subjectivity, and meaning continue to influence philosophical and musical thought today.
restrictions: Instructor permission is required for this course. Use the "Request Course Section Prerequisite Override" task found on your academics dashboard under the Planning & Registration section to request this permission.

title: Hildegard von Bingen
description: The course examines the music of twelfth-century composer, poet, saint, and doctor of the church Hildegard von Bingen by placing it within the context of her biography and her holistic creativity in areas such as theology, hagiography, natural science, medicine, language, and letters. The course will also explore aspects of notation, transmission, performance practice, chronology, and differing interpretations of the surviving—often contradictory—evidence concerning her biography and output.
restrictions: Instructor permission is required for this course. Use the "Request Course Section Prerequisite Override" task found on your academics dashboard under the Planning & Registration section to request this permission.

title: Vienna 1900
description: This course aims to provide students with an advanced musical and aesthetic understanding of late Romanticism and early modernism as they coexisted primarily in Vienna in the years around 1900. Starting with late nineteenth-century works by Brahms, Bruckner, and Wolf, the course focuses on transformations in compositional practice as evinced in the music of Mahler, Strauss, Schoenberg, and others. Through close analysis and score study, we will identify continuities with and departures from the formal conventions and artistic presuppositions of nineteenth-century Austro-German music. We will complement our musical studies with explorations of the social, political, philosophical, and psychological tensions in Viennese life at the time. The ultimate objective of the course—aside from offering students many occasions for powerful aesthetic and intellectual experiences—is to improve studentsʼ comprehension of both the stylistic details of (mostly) Vienna-based music circa 1900 and how that music engaged with concurrent historical and cultural trends.
restrictions: Instructor permission is required for this course. Use the "Request Course Section Prerequisite Override" task found on your academics dashboard under the Planning & Registration section to request this permission.

title: Cultures of Improvisation, 1770-1870
description: This course explores how European musicians, poets, critics, listeners, and novelists both practiced and understood improvisation throughout the long nineteenth century. Many of the important shifts in musical culture during this period—including the emergence of the work concept and the increasing importance of the score as object of reverence—have been invoked to explain improvisationʼs decline or marginalization by the turn of the twentieth century. We will critically evaluate such broad changes, asking whether they truly did act to suppress improvisation and if so, how. Focusing on improvisation in poetry, opera, and keyboard performance, as well as critical reception of virtuosi and improvisers, we will move beyond both the musical scene and musicology as a discipline to trace improvisationʼs importance in wider cultural contexts. In addition to a final research project, students can expect to engage in readings, discussion, presentations, and classroom activities.
restrictions: Instructor permission is required for this course. Use the "Request Course Section Prerequisite Override" task found on your academics dashboard under the Planning & Registration section to request this permission.

title: Barbara Strozzi and Her World
description: Barbara Strozzi (1619-1677) was one of the most gifted composers of the baroque period, the author of a sizable body of sacred and secular works. Recent cultural and musical scholarship—as well as frequent performance—has clarified the nature of her achievement, as well as the challenges she faced, which were similar for many women who sought artistic prominence. We will explore both the nature and circumstances of Strozziʼs work, including her madrigals, motets, arias, and (especially) cantatas, and we will chart what is known of her biography, trying to understand the place of music in her life. We will also compare her achievements to those of other prominent women of the period, both musical and otherwise. A final research project, presented to the seminar, will be required and may consider Strozzi and/or her “world” more generally.
restrictions: Instructor permission is required for this course. Use the "Request Course Section Prerequisite Override" task found on your academics dashboard under the Planning & Registration section to request this permission.

title: Creating a Modern Music Career
description: ONLY FOR MASTER OF ARTS IN MUSIC LEADERSHIP STUDENTS What does a successful career look like to you? What are some pursuits you wish to accomplish in your life? Can your career help you be mentally and emotionally fulfilled? In Creating a Modern Music Career, we will utilize the Design Your Life model to discover what will work for you on both a professional and personal level. Through class activities and weekly assignments, you will reflect upon your values and definition of success, explore options for your future, and understand the steps you'll need to take to accomplish your goals. As a course that is based on individualization, all tasks are designed to directly support your specific interests and pursuits. At the end of this course, you will have: 1. a list of relevant resources (job boards, training programs, salary databases, etc.), 2. drafted application materials (resume, cover letters, bios, etc.) 3. talked directly to professionals in the fields you are interested in, and 4. designed an individualized strategy outlining steps for 3 potential career/personal journeys. The instructors for the class are Institute for Music Leadership career advisors Dr. Blaire Koerner & Jeff Dunn. In addition to their advising roles, both are trained musicians (Eastman alums!) that collectively have experience being faculty in higher education, educators in public school districts, freelancers and orchestral subs, running their ensembles, starting a podcast, and more!
restrictions: Open to Major only - Music Leadership

title: Global Musicianship as Performer, Teacher and Leader
description: ONLY FOR MASTER OF ARTS IN MUSIC LEADERSHIP STUDENTS This coursewill look at how equity and inclusion work dictate the work of a musician as a performer, teacher, and leader of a music organization. The class will be divided into 3 sections – Performer, Teacher, Leader, while investigating closely how those roles may shift and how that should inform decision-making and implementation of equity and inclusion efforts.
restrictions: Open to Major only - Music Leadership

title: Building Your Audience
description: ONLY FOR MASTER OF ARTS IN MUSIC LEADERSHIP STUDENTS Interested in being a freelance musician? Running your own educational studio? Starting a chamber group? Being commissioned to compose or arrange music? Then you need to be well-versed in self-promotion! Many creative types think of marketing and public relations as a necessary evil in making a business out of being themselves. But when you think of marketing and communications as a natural extension of your own identity as a musician, the practice evolves to become a dialogue that fosters community through storytelling with your fans, helping them connect to something that they love and adds meaning to their lives. In this course, youʼll explore ways to create an integrated marketing strategy and tactical plan that will help you do just that, examining topics such as branding, the consumer journey, media relations, influencer marketing, social media, video, Google Analytics, and more.
restrictions: Open to Major only - Music Leadership

title: Econ Musical Arts Organization
description: Students will generate and filter their own entrepreneurial ideas and evaluate them based on technical and artistic merit, business challenges, and market indicators. Students will research their ideas, complete a market analysis, and meet with successful guest entrepreneurs in the music industry. This course outlines a critical process used by successful innovators to evaluate new venture ideas in any setting. Students will design and present a prototype of their idea in a public event for testing and feedback.
restrictions: Open to Major only - Music Leadership

title: Marketing for Musical Enterprises
description: Students will develop a foundational knowledge of marketing principles and strategies related to music and arts organizations, with an emphasis on current and emerging digital media. Students will use analytic tools and develop strategies for creating and sustaining meaningful, relevant, and artistically valuable programming and activities to engage the communities in which musical enterprises operate. Individually and in teams, the class will learn to think systematically about business problems, recognize the major forces that shape the decision-making environment, and understand how these forces affect and are affected by marketing activities. The course takes the view that business challenges facing arts organizations can be met with the same basic tools and principles used by organizations in any for-profit or not-for-profit industry.

title: Sophomore Review
restrictions: Instructor permission is required for this course. Use the "Request Course Section Prerequisite Override" task found on your academics dashboard under the Planning & Registration section to request this permission.

title: Intro to Music Teaching & Learning II
description: This course is Part Two of the MTL 110-111 sequence. Students will participate in a series of lectures by faculty in theDepartment of Music Teaching and Learningat Eastman and other specialists on topics relevant to music teaching and learning. The remaining hours of required field experience for the MTL 110-111 sequence (15 hours total) are completed during MTL 111.

description: This course is Part Two of the MTL 110-111 sequence. title: Pre-Internship Review
restrictions: Instructor permission is required for this course. Use the "Request Course Section Prerequisite Override" task found on your academics dashboard under the Planning & Registration section to request this permission.

title: Secondary General Music Methods
description: This course is designed to prepare students for teaching general music to all secondary age students, regardless of socioeconomic status, ability, or previous musical experience. Examines the importance of music education to an educated citizenry. Technology for music composition and music production is incorporated throughout. Observation and guided teaching experiences emphasize age-appropriate communication and classroom management as well as instructional planning and assessment (formal and informal). Reflective assignments for the teaching portfolio are encouraged. At least 15 hours of field experience are required.
restrictions: Non-MUE majors eligible with permission

title: Elementary/Middle School Choral Methods
description: In this course, pre-service teachers will develop increased proficiency as instructors in vocal music classrooms for students in grades 4-8. These proficiencies include knowledge and skills in group vocal pedagogy, music repertoire selection, curriculum design, differentiated instruction, classroom management, and communication. Extensive observation and teaching experiences take place in inclusive vocal music classrooms where assistive technology is frequently employed. In the classroom, pre-service teachers have opportunities to see how parents, teachers, professional staff, and administrators interact productively to enhance student learning. Reflective assignments for the teaching portfolio are encouraged and at least 15 hours of field experience are required.

title: Music for Special Learners
description: This course explores the nature of music education for students within the full range of disabilities and special-health care needs identified by the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA). Through class sessions, readings, observation and guided teaching, preservice teachers will identify the characteristics of special learners and observe how these affect musical behaviors. Strategies will be developed for (a) modifying existing music materials, (b) developing original music materials, and (c) collaborating with other professionals to design appropriate music learning environments and enhance the overall education of special learners. 10 hours of field experience will be required as part of this course.

title: Elementary Winds/Brass/Percussion Methods
description: This course allows pre-service teachers to develop an understanding of research-validated, appropriate methods for teaching elementary instrumental music (what to teach, when to teach, and why), and to develop the necessary techniques (how to teach) to implement those methods. The principles of music literacy acquisition and language acquisition are compared. Course requirements include: facility with function-based rhythm and tonal syllables, the ability to teach and coach composition and improvisation activities with elementary instrumental students (implementing appropriate technology), measurement and evaluation of instrumental teaching and performance skills. At least 15 hours of field experience allows pre-service teachers opportunity to observe how productive relationships among the school, home, and community can enhance students' music learning.

title: Secondary Instrument Rehearsal: WBP
description: This course allows pre-service teachers to develop an understanding of research-validated, appropriate methods for teaching secondary instrumental music, and to develop the necessary techniques to implement those methods. Course requirements include: making long-and short-range instructional plans based on a systematic analysis of the performance of individuals and the ensemble, teaching individual and small group lessons, rehearsing and conducting small and large ensembles. Assignments incorporate the use of music composition and production technologies. Through a minimum of 15 hours of field experience, pre-service teachers have opportunity to interact with teachers, professional staff, parents and administrators to enhance the music learning of secondary school students and learn age-appropriate communication, and assessment (formal and informal) techniques.

title: Secondary Instrument Rehearsal: Strings
description: This course allows pre-service teachers to develop an understanding of research-validated, appropriate methods for teaching secondary instrumental music, and to develop the necessary techniques to implement those methods. Course requirements include: making long-and short-range instructional plans based on a systematic analysis of the performance of individuals and the ensemble, teaching private and small group lessons, rehearsing and conducting the large ensemble. Through a minimum of 25 hours of field experience, pre- service teachers have opportunity to interact with teachers, professional staff, parents and administrators to enhance the music learning of high school students and learn age- appropriate classroom management, communication, and assessment (formal and informal) techniques. Video recording, reflective analysis and subsequent modification of all supervised teaching episodes in the field are required.

title: Clarinet Class
description: A laboratory class that allows students to develop intermediate-level performance skills on the instrument and methods for teaching. In preparation for teaching, students will learn to diagnose common performance problems associated with the clarinet and prescribe a variety of appropriate solutions. Additional topics covered include: maintenance and emergency repair, equipment and accessories, method books and repertoire, extended range and fingerings, and auxiliary clarinets. 2 hours field experience required.
restrictions: Non-MUE majors eligible with permission

title: Woodwind Class
description: Focuses on the development of intermediate-level performance skills and methods for teaching on a specific woodwind instrument; flute, oboe, saxophone, bassoon.
restrictions: Non-MUE majors eligible with permission

title: Trumpet Class
description: A laboratory class that allows students to develop intermediate level performance skills on the instrument and methods for reaching. In preparation for teaching, students will learn to diagnose common performance problems associated with the trumpet and prescribe a variety of appropriate solutions. Additional topics covered include: maintenance and emergency repair, equipment and accessories, method books and repertoire, embouchure problems, and auxiliary trumpets. 2 hours field experience required.
restrictions: Non-MUE majors eligible with permission

title: Strings Class II
description: Development of string playing techniques on two stringed instruments. Includes one upper and one lower stringed instrument. 1 hour of field experience will be required as part of this course.
restrictions: Non-MUE majors eligible with permission

title: Voice Class II
description: Extension of Voice Class I, with opportunity to continue to develop individual skills in singing. 1 hour field experience required.
restrictions: Non-MUE majors eligible with permission

title: Percussion Class
description: A laboratory class that allows students to develop intermediate level performance skills on the instruments of the percussion family as well as addressing methods for teaching. In preparation for teaching, students will learn to diagnose common performance problems associated with percussion instruments and prescribe a variety of appropriate solutions. Additional topics covered include: maintenance and emergency repair, equipment and accessories, and method books and repertoire. 2 hours field experience required.
restrictions: Non-MUE majors eligible with permission

title: Principles of String Playing and Teaching II
description: A continuation of MTL/PED 285 with a focus on procedures for establishing a private studio, reviewing and developing a collection of method books, etude books, and solo materials. Students present case studies of their school-age string students to whom they continue to teach weekly private lessons to students at ROCmusic.

title: Introduction to Research in Music Education
description: This course is designed for graduate students to develop an overview of the existing published research in music education. Content includes vocabulary and concepts related to quantitative and qualitative research designs in music education. Daily/weekly assignments to critique and analyze studies lead to a final review of literature paper and presentation.

title: Instrumental Methods and Techniques: WBP
description: For instrumental, vocal, and general music teachers at all levels who wish to improve their musicianship skill for teaching, this course emphasizes innovative ways to address State and National Standards in Music and appropriately differentiate music instruction. The principles of music literacy acquisition and language acquisition are compared. Assignments incorporate the use of music composition and production technologies. 25 hours of field experience are required for students pursuing Initial-Professional Certification.

title: Instrumental Methods and Techniques: Strings
description: For instrumental, vocal, and general music teachers at all levels who wish to improve their musicianship skill for teaching, this course provides an orientation to the design and implementation of string programs, with emphasis on developing age appropriate instructional strategies, classroom management, communication and assessment (formal and informal) for heterogeneous groups. The principles of music literacy acquisition and language acquisition are compared. Video recording, reflective analysis and subsequent modification of all supervised teaching episodes in the field are required. Assignments incorporate the use of music composition and production technologies. 25 hours of field experience are required for students pursuing Initial-Professional Certification.

title: Teaching Internship: General
description: Students are required to demonstrate competence in teaching and the application of concepts presented in other courses required by the MM or MA degrees in music education by submitting the following: (1) audio and video tapes of classes, (2) course descriptions and outlines, (3) sample examinations, (4) an annual calendar of performances and activities, and/or (5) sample programs. Students who are also employed as teachers can submit materials developed for their own classes; full-time graduate students are assigned a teaching responsibility to complete the requirements under faculty supervision. Readings are assigned individually. This requirement may be satisfied in one of the following ways: (1) one- on-one with a music education faculty member; (2) collaborative teacher study group; (3) review of a substantial portfolio documenting teaching competence.

title: MA Field Project in MUE
description: The specific nature of this master's project will be developed in consultation with a faculty member in the Music Education Department. Guidelines are available in the department for project proposals, which must be approved by the faculty. Examples might include (a) a field-based research study within a teacher's own classroom setting, (b) a curriculum project, or (c) a small-scale replication of an existing research study with a new population. At the completion of the project, a student will submit a written report, again subject to approval by the entire faculty. Please see Guidelines for Field Project for complete information. Parallel to the registration for thesis credits, the registration for this master's project may be broken down into single credits or enrolled as a block of four credits.
restrictions: Instructor permission is required for this course. Use the "Request Course Section Prerequisite Override" task found on your academics dashboard under the Planning & Registration section to request this permission.

title: Improvisation
description: For graduate students with varied backgrounds in improvisation, including those with limited experience, this class will introduce practical techniques designed to: (1) develop improvisation skill in various styles; (2) include improvisation as an integral part of performance and instruction; and (3) define relationships between improvising, reading, and composing music. The class will explore a variety of performance genres and include principles for teaching improvisation and learning to improvise. Course content includes a logical harmonic and rhythmic sequence for skill development. Students will learn repertoire by ear, transcribe solos, and improvise solos, accompaniments, and counterpoint.

title: MA Thesis Project

title: Curriculum Seminar
description: Inquiry into curriculum theory and creative curriculum development and implementation. Attention is devoted to how schools are organized, how processes and outcomes of learning are evaluated, and how conditions can be created to foster professional growth among music teachers and administrators.

title: Preparing Future Music Faculty
description: All musicians participate in various forms of music teaching and learning. For those graduate students who desire to teach in a college or community music school, even on a part-time basis, this course will prepare you to teach effectively. During the course, you will begin to develop a teaching portfolio that will complement your performance portfolio. You will also explore ways to organize music content for learning, assess your studentsʼ prior musical knowledge and experience, communicate expectations to your students, and speak knowledgeably about teaching with colleagues and administrators. RESTRICTED TO GRADUATE STUDENTS ONLY. Graduate students wishing to take this course for degree elective credit will need to pay for the course and should sign up for MTL 504.

title: Introduction to Ethnomusicology
description: This course explores key concepts, theories, and case studies from the history of ethnomusicology from the end of the 19th-century through the present day. We will examine shifts in understanding the relationship between music and culture, the meaning of identity, and applications of fieldwork in increasingly globalized and urbanized areas. Ethnomusicology has a rich transdisciplinary orientation, and we will therefore engage substantively with works from other fields including anthropology, linguistics, and comparative literature. Lastly, as a field concerned with the history and practice of musics from other cultures, this course will provide exposure to a range of theories, traditions, and sounds from around the world.
restrictions: Open to Major only - Musicology

title: German Romanticism, Then and Now
description: The question “What is Romanticism?” is notoriously difficult to answer. Although musicians commonly associate Romanticism with the music of the entire nineteenth century (especially that of the German-speaking lands), the term also refers to literary and philosophical trends that were essentially defunct by 1848, when revolutionary fervor and new perspectives swept across Europe. In keeping with this more restricted timescale, we will seek to gain a better understanding of German Romanticism as an early nineteenth-century phenomenon grounded in ideas that took shape as much around music (or with music as their object) as within it. We will closely study works of music by composers such as Beethoven, Schubert, and the Schumanns that continue to be heard as illustrative of Romantic aesthetics or that invite Romantic listening attitudes, asking what technical features of the music justify these convictions. Finally, through provocative pairings of historical and contemporary readings by authors such as E.T.A. Hoffmann, Andrea Wulf, Brian Massumi, Dalia Nassar, G.W.F. Hegel, Arthur Schopenhauer, Isaiah Berlin, and Hartmut Rosa, we will consider how Romantic notions of nature, subjectivity, and meaning continue to influence philosophical and musical thought today.

title: Hildegard von Bingen
description: The course examines the music of twelfth-century composer, poet, saint, and doctor of the church Hildegard von Bingen by placing it within the context of her biography and her holistic creativity in areas such as theology, hagiography, natural science, medicine, language, and letters. The course will also explore aspects of notation, transmission, performance practice, chronology, and differing interpretations of the surviving—often contradictory—evidence concerning her biography and output.

title: Sacred Music Skills II
description: Course will build upon skill development from OSH 201-401 in singing, conducting, and rehearsal techniques. Students will create a curriculum vitae, design components of a church music program, and plan liturgies based upon the Revised Common Lectionary. Students will prepare to accompany and direct Anglican Chant and Plainsong as well as to compose a choral settings as a vehicle for improvisational singing. Focus will be given to building and enhancing a parish music program.

title: Organ Improvisation
description: The purpose of this course is to develop skills and techniques in musical improvisation, beginning with harmonization of hymns and chorales and progressing to work in building skills in a variety of genres and styles. Sections consist of semi-private lessons in small groups of 2-4 students.

title: Organ History, Design, and Maintenance
description: This course will cover pipe organ functionality and design to include the major historic schools of organ building. It will also include practical tuning and maintenance techniques and “hands on” participation in a pipe organ restoration and installation project. This class meets weekly as a group for one hour, with one additional hour per week of lab to be scheduled based on the studentʼs availability. Required for undergraduate organ majors; open to others by permission of the instructor.
restrictions: Open to Primary Instrument - Organ

title: Organ Colloquium
description: Organ Department Colloquium: Each Monday evening, the entire organ department gathers for a session devoted to relevant professional topics and practical work in aspects of sacred music practice. Some sessions involve a guest master class or lecture, as well as faculty and student presentations. Also, over the course of one academic year, each student prepares a hymn to play on the organ, while the class and faculty function as the congregation. Likewise, each student prepares a choral anthem with organ accompaniment, and rehearses the class and faculty as if they were a choir, and then conducts and accompanies simultaneously a performance. Consequently, each student performs one hymn and one anthem per year for the helpful feedback of colleagues and teachers. Colloquium is held in Schmitt Hall, but sometimes at Memorial Art Gallery or a local church in order to use those instruments and spaces.
restrictions: Open to Primary Instrument - Organ

title: Expanding the Repertoire of the Modern Pianist
description: PED 262 will focus on the alternate choices of repertoire, with particular focus on an exploration of Diversity in Repertoire, an increasingly important topic in the field of music. Each of these topics would be covered in 2-3week increments. This syllabus explore selected piano works at varying levels of difficulty (late elementary to advanced), surveyed from a pedagogical and performance perspective. The course will be almost totally online, with the 1stand last classes in-person; topics include An Exploration of Pedagogical Composers; Teaching and performing Jazz; Exploring repertoire, from Baroque through contemporary, by Women, Asian, Latin-American, and other composers. The class will end with a discussion of new kinds of programming for the 21stcentury pianist.

title: Secondary Piccolo
description: This course will serve as an introduction to some of the key themes that emerge in texts from the so-called “Golden Age of Athens.” Our goal will be to become familiar with the preoccupations of the ancient Greeks (who of course, did not understand themselves as “ancient”). Our topics will include the nature of rationalist and “poetic” approaches, and the tensions between them. We will also examine the development of Athenian democracy, its promises and its pathologies. Finally, we will also embark on a series of projects designed to play with or re-work the Greeks so as to bring them into conversation with the preoccupations of the present.
restrictions: Instructor permission is required for this course. Use the "Request Course Section Prerequisite Override" task found on your academics dashboard under the Planning & Registration section to request this permission.

title: Marx, Dostoevsky, Nietzsche, and Freud
description: Sometime near the beginning of the 19th century, the world began to undergo a series of massive social, cultural, and political transformations: These include, among other things, urbanization, industrialization, secularization, the growth of representative democracy and the nation-state, and the move away from traditional structures of thought to the belief that human beings could improve their lot through the use of rational, technical, and scientific practices. Using the writings of Marx, Dostoevsky, Nietzsche, and Freud as our guide, this course will explore some of these transformations and their implications: Is the growth of rationality the same as “progress,” or does it encode new and more pernicious forms of domination? Are the freedoms that emerge with capitalism and representative government complete, or do they guarantee (and mask) exploitation? As traditional metaphysical worldviews collapse, what are the prospects for creating a meaningful life, and what are the political, cultural, and social consequences if we cannot do so? In addition to reading texts and talking them, we will also engage in a series of projects that explore these questions.

title: Educational Psychology
description: This course will focus on theories of child development, learning, and motivation, building on concepts introduced in the general psychology course. Students will explore a variety of theories and apply them to educational situations to achieve a deeper understanding of how children develop as learners. The course will center on a range of concepts, both cognitive and social, and on ways students might use these to become more insightful, sensitive, and skilled as educators. Open to Music Ed majors only.
restrictions: Non-MUE majors eligible with permission

title: Schola Cantorum
description: SMU 210 (I,II-1) Schola Cantorum This consort of voices and instruments performs weekly in the liturgical candlelight setting of Compline. We explore known practices in performing music from the Renaissance to the Romantic era, as well as Gregorian Chant, choral improvisation, and new music composed especially for this ensemble. In addition to voices, instruments include sackbuts, cornetti, strings, and organ. The 30-minute public performances take place each Sunday evening at 9:00 p.m. from October through April in the exceptional acoustic of Christ Church. Rehearsals precede the performance on Sunday evenings from 7:30 - 8:45 p.m. Ensemble is comprised of faculty, students, and community professionals. Audition required with instructor Stephen Kennedy. Preferred email: stephenk@rochester.rr.com
restrictions: Instructor permission is required for this course. Use the "Request Course Section Prerequisite Override" task found on your academics dashboard under the Planning & Registration section to request this permission.

title: Schola Cantorum
description: This honors course for advanced students progresses more rapidly through topics of the undergraduate core curriculum, enriching theory study with additional projects in composition, keyboard study, and analytical topics. The class is a continuation of TH 101H.
restrictions: Instructor permission is required for this course. Use the "Request Course Section Prerequisite Override" task found on your academics dashboard under the Planning & Registration section to request this permission.

title: Music Analysis & Style Composition I
description: This course provides an introduction to harmony and counterpoint, with a primary focus on how these musical elements behave and interact in music of the European “common practice” period (ca. 1770–1900). The course begins with species counterpoint exercises in which students examine relationships between melodies, bass lines, and underlying harmonic progressions. After an overview of phrase-level forms, the bulk of the course explores the harmonic syntax of this music, primarily through the lens of harmonic function. The aim throughout is for students to hear, recognize, and reproduce the most idiomatic progressions that composers used within a single key—and to master the syntactic rules that undergird these progressions.

title: Aural Musicianship I
description: The primary goal of aural musicianship study at the Eastman School of Music is to know musical space as comfortably and intuitively as one knows the shapes and contours of a familiar room. Musicianship training enables musicians to map sound to symbol, symbol to sound, to process what they are hearing in real time, and to remain oriented in musical space. The first course in the sequence will emphasize strategies for accurate and expressive reading, improvisation, singing, and dictation using elementary rhythms and diatonic pitch materials in treble and bass clefs.
